title: Preparing Antibiotics Stocks
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"6b65e","text":"Plasmids can carry one or more antibiotic resistance genes, which confer resistance to a specific antibiotic to the bacteria carrying them. The presence of an antibiotic resistance gene on a plasmid allows researchers to easily isolate bacteria containing that plasmid from bacteria that do not contain it by artificial selection (i.e. growing the bacteria in the presence of the antibiotic).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: A B C 1 Antibiotic Stock [M] Working [M] 2 Ampicillin 100 mg/mL 100 µg/mL 3 Bleocin 5 mg/mL 5 µg/mL 4 Carbenicillin* 100 mg/mL 100 µg/mL 5 Chloramphenicol 25 mg/mL (dissolve in EtOH) 25 µg/mL 6 Coumermycin 25 mg/mL (dissolve in DMSO) 25 µg/mL 7 Gentamycin 10 mg/mL 10 µg/mL 8 Kanamycin 50 mg/mL 50 µg/mL 9 Spectinomycin 50 mg/mL 50 µg/mL 10 Tetracycline 10 mg/mL 10 µg/mL Unless otherwise indicated, the antibiotic powder can be dissolved in dH2O. *Carbenicillin can be used in place of ampicillin. Carbenicillin is more stable, so it is potentially more effective at selecting only bacteria containing the plasmids of interest (for example, fewer satellite colonies will grow). However, it is also more expensive. Cite: https://www.addgene.org/protocols/pouring-lb-agar-plates/
In a 15 mL falcon tube make the stock antibiotics. Make 10 mL at a time.
Aliquot 1mL of the antibiotics into 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes.
Label the tubes with the antibiotic and concentration.
Store in the antibiotics box in the -20 C freezer.
creator: Tamra Mbeuh Lahom Lot
